data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "No fixed precision/recall regime suffices; harness strictly beats self-consistency only when critic confidence is question-adaptive such that the critic vetoes wrong-majority sets at high confidence and abstains on right-majority sets, making the effective per-question FPR near zero while maintaining nonzero recall on wrong sets.","mechanism": "The repository's data show that a single global critic decision (veto/allow) with fixed precision 0.125 and recall 1.0 yields zero net benefit because the critic flags everything, so filtering is a no-op on the already-correct majority. The causal failure is not the critic's recall but its inability to discriminate which veto signals are reliable. If instead the critic outputs a calibrated confidence p_veto and the harness vetoes only when p_veto > threshold tau, then at high tau the critic abstains on most right-majority questions (FPR -> 0) while still catching wrong-majority questions where the evidence is strong. The strict advantage regime is therefore not a point in (base error, precision, recall, k) space but a curve: for any base error in (0.5-p, 0.5+p) where p scales with 1/sqrt(k), there exists a threshold tau making the expected net-fixed-minus-broke positive if and only if the critic's score distribution for wrong-majority sets stochastically dominates its score distribution for right-majority sets.","scope": {"covers": ["critic outputs calibrated confidence, not binary veto", "thresholded veto policy with tunable tau", "base error 0.40-0.70", "any k >= 5"], "excludes": ["binary veto critic with fixed global precision/recall", "critic score distributions for right and wrong majorities are identical"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Critic score distributions for right-majority and wrong-majority sets are indistinguishable (KS test p > 0.05) yet thresholded harness strictly beats self-consistency on >= 50% of questions", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Critic score distributions are separable (KS p < 0.05) but no threshold tau yields positive net-fixed-minus-broke averaged over the test set", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "The optimal-threshold harness accuracy exceeds self-consistency but the binary-veto harness accuracy also exceeds self-consistency by the same margin, contradicting that adaptivity is necessary", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}],"prose_notes": "Experiment: reuse the repository's 44 questions plus 60 new questions spanning base error 0.30-0.70; generate k=5 candidates; instead of binary critic veto, prompt the critic to output a confidence score 0-1 for 'this candidate set's majority is wrong'; sweep tau in {0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 0.95}; compute per-tau harness accuracy and net-fixed-minus-broke; also run the binary-veto harness for comparison. Confirm: (1) at high tau, FPR on right-majority sets drops below 0.1 while recall on wrong-majority sets stays above 0.3; (2) there exists tau where harness strictly beats SC; (3) binary veto does not match this. Token budget: 104 q * 5 cand * 800 tokens + 104 q * 600 critic + 104 q * 5 thresholds * 50 eval + analysis ~= 86k."}
